sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.content: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is one of the most aggressive skin cancers, with an incidence of approximately 2,488 individuals affected per year in the United States [1]. The disease can grow rapidly and metastasize early, with 63% of primary lesions having grown rapidly in the 3 months prior to diagnosis [7]. Large meta-analyses have shown that at least half of patients with MCC develop lymph node metastases and nearly one-third develop distant metastases [8-15]. Several studies document the development of locoregional recurrence in up to half of all instances of MCC [14-20]. MCC has a high mortality rate, exceeding melanoma. The 5-year relative or MCC-specific survival rates range from 41% to 77%, depending on stage at presentation [2,10,13,18-23].

factors.detail: LVI is predictive of SLN positivity, recurrence, OS, and DSS. A large review (N=500) showed <1% versus 35% of those who died of MCC were LVI-negative and LVI-positive, respectively [22,72,75-78]

Large database analyses have found primary tumor size significantly associated with nodal involvement, DSS, and OS [11,13,49,68,79,82]. Risk of microscopic LN involvement is non-negligible even among patients in the smallest tumor size category [13,49,77,82,83]

Presence of TILs associated with improved survival outcomes. A retrospective cohort (N=2182) established that subdivision into nonbrisk and brisk TILs was associated with incrementally improved OS compared with no TILs [76,85-91]
Infiltrative growth pattern associated with higher risk of SLN positivity or poor outcomes compared with nodular pattern [76,83]

MCC is disproportionally more common in immunosuppressed individuals (organ transplant, CLL, HIV). MCC-specific survival is worse for those with immunosuppression in multiple studies [19,38,40-47,114]

MCPyV-negative tumors tend to occur more often on the head, neck, or trunk and have been associated with increased recurrence [55] and decreased MCC-specific survival and OS in some studies [57-60]. Genetic analyses found much higher mutational burden in MCPyV-negative tumors [63-65]

In the largest study (N=6901, NCDB), margin status was significantly associated with survival for MCC with stage I, II, or III [68]. Among those with stage I/II disease treated with surgery only, recurrence rates and OS were better for negative versus positive margins [205]
MCC incidence increases with age and is more likely to occur in white individuals compared with other ethnicities [4,7,8,13,19]

Prognosis worsens with increasing nodal involvement including clinically detectable vs microscopic, increasing number of nodes, and presence of extranodal extension [12,13,17,22,49,68,72,79,82,181,215,228,239,258,259]
Identified as an adverse risk factor for local recurrence [19,22,38,40-47,49,72,75-78,114]
Patients with MCC are at higher risk for developing second cancers including SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, NHLs, lung, breast, and kidney cancers [19,23,35,91,94,97-104]
